title: Missouri vs Miami — First Round Viewing Guide
eventTag: NCAA Tournament • First Round • St. Louis, MO
venue: Enterprise Center, St. Louis, Missouri • truTV
extraInfo: Winner faces #2 UCLA or #15 Queens on Sunday

stakes.callout: A 10-seed playing 100 miles from campus against a 7-seed that traveled 1,200 miles. Missouri has a de facto home game in St. Louis, a McDonald's All-American who came home from Duke, and seven players 6-9 or taller. Miami went from 7-24 to 25-8 in a single season — the largest turnaround in Division I basketball — under a 37-year-old first-time head coach who shows his team horror movie clips. Both teams win by pounding the paint. Something has to give.

cards.label: For Missouri
cards.value: Dennis Gates has taken Mizzou to three NCAA Tournaments in four years — the first time that's happened since 2012-13. But they haven't won a game yet. Mark Mitchell, a McDonald's All-American who left Duke to come home to Missouri, is playing his likely final college game 250 miles from where he grew up in Kansas City. The crowd will be theirs. The pressure is to finally break through.

cards.label: For Miami
cards.value: Eighteen months ago, Miami was at the Final Four. Then eight players entered the portal, the legendary coach retired mid-season, and they went 7-24. Jai Lucas rebuilt the entire roster in one offseason and went 25-8. A tournament win validates the most dramatic turnaround in college basketball. Three senior transfers — Reneau, Donaldson, Udeh — have extensive NCAA Tournament experience.

cards.label: The Home-Court Heist
cards.value: Missouri's campus in Columbia is just 100 miles from the Enterprise Center in St. Louis. Miami must travel 1,200+ miles. The committee handed Mizzou a de facto home game as a 10-seed. Despite being the lower seed, the Tigers will have a massive crowd advantage — one of the most lopsided geographic draws in the entire bracket.

cards.label: The Paint Battle
cards.value: Both teams are built to dominate the interior. Missouri ranks No. 2 nationally in free-throw rate and has seven players 6-9+. Miami ranks 331st in three-point attempts — they don't even try to shoot threes. This will be a physical, bruising, inside-out slugfest with very few three-pointers.

styles.title: Seven Guys Over 6-9 and a Mission
bullets: Elite free-throw generation: Missouri ranks No. 2 nationally in free-throw rate (BartTorvik). The offense is built around attacking the rim and drawing contact. Mark Mitchell is the engine — 18.3 PPG on .551 FG%, bulldozing to the basket and getting to the line.
Interior size: Seven players listed at 6-9 or taller — the most in school history. They ranked 3rd in the SEC in two-point defense. Shawn Phillips Jr. (7-0, .691 FG%) anchors the paint.

Trent Pierce changes everything: Missed the first half with injury. Since returning: 10.6 PPG on .404 from three (4.9 attempts per game). He's the only reliable perimeter shooter, and when he's hitting, Mitchell gets easier looks inside.

T.O. Barrett's breakout: The unranked three-star sophomore averaged 13.3 PPG, 3.6 APG, and 1.6 SPG in 12 starts. Career-high 28 points vs. Tennessee. His emergence as a starter transformed the backcourt.

Weakness: Defensive efficiency ranks 237th in KenPom. They can defend the paint but struggle on the perimeter. Never won more than two consecutive conference games. Jayden Stone shot 30.6% over the last five games — if he's cold, the offense gets one-dimensional.

styles.title: The Monster Makeover
bullets: From 7-24 to 25-8: The largest single-season turnaround in Division I basketball. Jai Lucas built a brand-new roster through the portal around one identity: size, physicality, toughness. The "Monster" culture — complete with a Friday the 13th hockey mask ritual after wins — is real.

Paint dominance: Malik Reneau (.549 FG%), Ernest Udeh Jr. (.735 FG%), and Shelton Henderson (.563 FG%) all finish at elite rates inside. Miami ranks top-20 nationally in rebounding metrics. They outscore you from the paint and the free-throw line.

Donaldson is the closer: Tre Donaldson averages 16.5 PPG and 5.8 APG. Career-high 32 points vs. Virginia Tech — scoring all of Miami's final 15 points in a 67-66 win. A proven clutch performer at his third school (Auburn, Michigan, Miami).
KenPom loves them: Ranked 31st overall with a 33rd-ranked offense (121.4 efficiency) and 38th-ranked defense (100.7). Far more efficient than their seed suggests.

Weakness: Ranked 331st nationally in three-point volume. If Missouri packs the paint with their length, Miami has no perimeter release valve. Udeh shoots .512 from the free-throw line — he's hackable in crunch time. Henderson (.588 FT%) is a problem too.

keys.title: Two paint-first teams, one paint.
keys.description: Missouri ranks No. 2 nationally in free-throw rate. Miami ranks 331st in three-point attempts. Neither team wants to shoot jumpers. Both want to pound the ball inside, draw fouls, and win at the rim. The team that controls the interior matchup controls the game. With seven players 6-9+ for Mizzou and Udeh (6-11, 250) plus Reneau (6-9, 233) for Miami, this is a battle of pure interior will. Expect a lot of whistles and a lot of contact.

keys.title: Can Missouri's crowd advantage overcome Miami's talent edge?
keys.description: The Enterprise Center will be a Mizzou home game. Columbia is 100 miles away; Miami traveled 1,200+ miles. In a tight game, that crowd matters — especially at the free-throw line (and both teams shoot a lot of free throws). But Miami is a 7-seed for a reason: KenPom 31st vs. Missouri's 51st. The Hurricanes are simply the better team on paper. The question is whether geography and emotion can close the gap.

keys.title: Mark Mitchell vs. Malik Reneau — the homecoming showdown.
keys.description: Mitchell (18.3 PPG, .551 FG%) came home from Duke to Missouri and is playing 250 miles from Kansas City. Reneau (18.8 PPG, .549 FG%) came home from Indiana to Miami. Both are physical, rim-attacking forwards who draw fouls at elite rates. Both are playing for their legacies. This head-to-head matchup will define the game. Whoever gets the other in foul trouble wins.

keys.title: Trent Pierce's three-point shooting vs. Miami's pack-the-paint defense.
keys.description: Pierce is shooting .404 from three since returning from injury. He's Missouri's only reliable perimeter shooter. If he's hitting, it forces Miami to extend their defense and opens driving lanes for Mitchell. If Pierce is cold, Miami can pack the paint with Udeh and Reneau and dare Missouri to shoot over them. Pierce may be the single most important player in this game, even though he isn't the best player on either team.

keys.title: The free-throw line decides it.
keys.description: Both teams generate tons of free throws. But Miami has a free-throw shooting problem: Udeh (.512) and Henderson (.588) are both liabilities at the line. Missouri shoots .686 as a team — not great either. In a game this physical, there will be 40+ free-throw attempts combined. The team that converts at a higher rate in the final five minutes will win. It's that simple.

prediction.summary: This is going to be a bruising, physical, low-three-point-attempt slugfest decided at the rim and the free-throw line. Both teams are paint-first — Missouri ranks #2 nationally in free-throw rate, Miami ranks 331st in three-point attempts — so expect a lot of whistles, a lot of contact, and a combined 50+ free-throw attempts. Missouri's de facto home crowd in St. Louis (just 100 miles from campus) gives them a real edge, especially at the free-throw line in a game this physical. But Miami is the better team on paper (KenPom 31st vs. Missouri's 51st), and Tre Donaldson's proven clutch gene — scoring all 15 of Miami's final points against Virginia Tech — gives the Hurricanes a closer that Missouri lacks. The key wildcard is Trent Pierce: if his three-point shot is falling (.404 since returning from injury), it opens driving lanes for Mitchell and forces Miami out of their pack-the-paint defense. If Pierce is cold, Miami can load up inside and smother Mitchell.

phases.title: Opening 10 Minutes
phases.description: The crowd is overwhelmingly gold and black as Missouri feeds off the St. Louis energy. Mitchell attacks the rim early and draws two quick fouls on Reneau. T.O. Barrett's physicality at 6-4, 205 surprises Miami's guards. Missouri leads 16-12 as both teams grind through half-court possessions.

phases.title: Mid-First Half to Halftime
phases.description: Miami's size starts asserting itself. Udeh cleans up on the offensive glass with a pair of putback dunks, and Donaldson finds his rhythm with a series of driving layups. Reneau scores through contact despite foul trouble. Pierce hits a three to keep Missouri's spacing alive, and the half ends 32-30 Miami in a rock fight.

phases.title: Second Half — The Physical War
phases.description: The foul count climbs. Both teams are in the bonus early in the second half, and free-throw shooting becomes the story. Udeh goes 1-for-4 at the line (.512 FT%) and Henderson misses a pair (.588 FT%). Missouri capitalizes at the stripe and ties it at 50 with 8 minutes left. Shelton Henderson's freshman physicality at 240 pounds is keeping Miami in it inside, but his free-throw woes limit his impact.

phases.title: Final 5 Minutes
phases.description: Donaldson takes over. The man who scored all of Miami's final 15 against Virginia Tech drives through Missouri's 237th-ranked defense for a pair of tough finishes. Mitchell answers with a contested jumper, but Reneau draws a foul on Shawn Phillips Jr. and sinks both free throws. The crowd wills Missouri back, but Donaldson hits the dagger — a pull-up jumper with 30 seconds left that seals it. Miami's "Monster" culture holds up under the hostile St. Louis atmosphere.

projectedScore.rationale: Miami's superior KenPom profile and Donaldson's closing ability edge out Missouri despite the hostile crowd. Free-throw shooting — Miami's weakness and the game's most common play — keeps it tight, but the Hurricanes' talent prevails in a physical, gritty affair.

players.name: Mark Mitchell
players.meta: F • 6-8 • Senior • McDonald's All-American, Duke Transfer
players.bio:
From Kansas City, Kansas. McDonald's All-American who scored a game-high 19 points in the 2022 all-star game. Five-star recruit who started 67 games at Duke over two seasons before coming home to Missouri. Career-high 31 points vs. then-No. 4 Alabama. Hit a buzzer-beating three in an OT win vs. Oklahoma. His 2.59-to-1 assist-to-turnover ratio in SEC play ranked 4th among all players and 1st among non-guards. Playing his likely final college game 250 miles from home.
Read more →

players.name: Jayden Stone
players.meta: G • 6-4 • Senior • From Perth, Australia • 4th College
players.bio:
From Perth, Australia. One of the most well-traveled players in college basketball: Grand Canyon → Detroit Mercy → West Virginia → Missouri. Averaged nearly 21 PPG at Detroit Mercy on a 1-31 team. Suffered a preseason injury at West Virginia and never played a game. Finally found stability at Mizzou. The concern: 6.6 PPG on 30.6% shooting over the last five games. If his late-season slump carries into March, Missouri's offense becomes too dependent on Mitchell.
Read more →

players.name: T.O. Barrett
players.bio:
From Oklahoma City. Led Edmond North HS to back-to-back Oklahoma state titles, earning championship game MVP honors. Arrived at Missouri as an unranked three-star recruit. The breakout player of the season: when he entered the starting lineup, everything changed. Career-high 28 points in a victory over Tennessee. Viewed as Missouri's point guard of the future. At 6-4 and 205 lbs, he has the size and strength to compete physically with Miami's backcourt.
Read more →

players.name: Trent Pierce
players.meta: F • 6-10 • Junior • Back from Injury, Mizzou's Best Shooter
players.bio:
From Tulsa, Oklahoma. Named National Sixth Man of the Year at AZ Compass Prep. ESPN's No. 60 recruit — Mizzou's highest-ranked incoming player since 2017. Missed the first half of the season with a lower-body injury. Since returning: .404 from three on 4.9 attempts per game in 16 games. He's the key to Missouri's spacing — when he hits, Mitchell gets cleaner looks inside. When he doesn't, Miami can pack the paint.
Read more →

players.name: Malik Reneau
players.meta: F • 6-9, 233 lbs • Senior • All-ACC First Team
players.bio:
From Miami, Florida. Won back-to-back GEICO National titles at Montverde Academy. Former top-30 recruit who spent three seasons at Indiana before entering the portal and coming home. The homecoming narrative is the story: a Miami kid who went north to the Big Ten and returned to lead his hometown team. Three career all-conference honors across two schools. The interior engine of everything Lucas runs.
Read more →

players.name: Tre Donaldson
players.meta: G • 6-2 • Senior • All-ACC Second Team • 3rd School
players.bio:
Originally recruited as a football prospect but chose basketball at Auburn. Won the 2024 SEC Tournament title. Transferred to Michigan — started all 37 games, helped the Wolverines win the 2025 Big Ten Tournament and reach the Sweet 16. Transferred to Miami for his final year. Career-high 32 points vs. Virginia Tech — scoring all of Miami's final 15 in a 67-66 win. Plays 85%+ of Miami's minutes. A journeyman who found his peak at his third school.
Read more →

players.name: Ernest Udeh Jr.
players.meta: C • 6-11, 250 lbs • Senior • ACC All-Defensive Team
players.bio:
From Orlando, Florida. McDonald's All-American. Began at Kansas, where he set the school record with 21 consecutive made field goals. Transferred to TCU (started all 59 games over two seasons). Now anchoring Miami's defense with 9.3 RPG (2nd in ACC) and 1.4 BPG (7th). His .735 FG% reflects a pure paint finisher who only takes shots he can make. The Achilles' heel: .512 free-throw shooting.
Read more →

players.name: Shelton Henderson
players.meta: F • 6-6, 240 lbs • Freshman • Five-Star, Houston POY
players.bio:
From Bellaire, Texas. 2025 Houston High School Player of the Year. Five-star recruit rated No. 23 overall in his class. Was Jai Lucas's first five-star recruit at Miami — Lucas recruited him while still at Duke. At 240 pounds as a freshman, he's one of the most physically imposing first-years in the country. Best when attacking downhill. NBA draft prospect. His three-point shot (.271) and free-throw shooting (.588) need work, but his physicality fits the "Monster" identity perfectly.
Read more →

funFacts.title: The Friday the 13th Speech (Miami)
funFacts.body: Before Miami's first game, Jai Lucas showed his team the scene from the 2009 Friday the 13th reboot where Jason Voorhees discovers his hockey mask. He asked: "What kind of monster do you want to be?" After each win, a player who embodies team values wears a hockey mask ceremonially. The "Monster" identity stuck.

funFacts.title: First Journalism School in the World (Missouri)
funFacts.body: The University of Missouri is home to the world's first School of Journalism, founded by Walter Williams in 1908. The Missouri Method — learning by doing through a working newsroom — became the model for journalism education worldwide.

funFacts.title: The Largest Turnaround in D-I (Miami)
funFacts.body: Miami went from 7-24 to 25-8 in one season — an 18-win improvement, the largest single-season turnaround in Division I basketball. Jim Larrañaga retired mid-season amid a 4-8 start. Jai Lucas rebuilt the entire roster through the transfer portal in a single offseason.

funFacts.title: Civil War Tigers (Missouri)
funFacts.body: The Tiger nickname dates to 1890, adopted in honor of a Civil War militia unit of ~120 Columbia citizens who defended the town from Confederate guerrilla bands. Truman the Tiger is named after Harry S. Truman — the only Missouri-born president.

funFacts.title: Football Prospect Turned Hoops Star (Miami)
funFacts.body: Tre Donaldson was originally recruited as a football prospect before choosing basketball at Auburn. He won the 2024 SEC Tournament title, helped Michigan reach the 2025 Sweet 16, and now leads Miami with 16.5 PPG and 5.8 APG at his third school.

funFacts.title: Jefferson's Tombstone on Campus (Missouri)
funFacts.body: Thomas Jefferson's original tombstone stands on the Missouri campus — a gift from his heirs in 1883. The university, founded in 1839, was the first public university west of the Mississippi River.

funFacts.title: Why an Ibis? (Miami)
funFacts.body: Sebastian the Ibis was chosen because the white ibis is the last wildlife to take shelter before a hurricane and the first to reappear afterward — symbolizing bravery and resilience. Originally named "Icky," the mascot was later renamed after San Sebastian Hall.

funFacts.title: Sheryl Crow, Jon Hamm, Sam Walton (Missouri)
funFacts.body: Mizzou alumni include Sheryl Crow (Grammy-winning musician), Jon Hamm (Emmy-winning actor), and Sam Walton (founder of Walmart). Also: the surgeon who founded the Mayo Clinic (William Worrall Mayo).

funFacts.title: The Rock Went to "The U" (Miami)
funFacts.body: Dwayne "The Rock" Johnson graduated from the University of Miami in 1995 with a degree in criminology and physiology. Other famous alumni: Gloria Estefan, Ray Liotta, and Sylvester Stallone (who attended but didn't graduate).

funFacts.title: The Last Time They Met (2002)
funFacts.body: Missouri and Miami have played once before in the NCAA Tournament — in 2002, when 12-seed Missouri upset 5-seed Miami (FL) in the first round. Missouri went on to reach the Elite Eight that year. Twenty-four years later, they meet again.

funFacts.title: Homecomings (Both Teams)
funFacts.body: Both teams have homecoming stories. Mark Mitchell left Duke to return to his home state of Kansas — now playing 250 miles from KC. Malik Reneau left Indiana to return to his hometown of Miami. Dante Allen, son of NBA vet Malik Allen, stayed home in Miami.

funFacts.title: The Geographic Heist (Missouri)
funFacts.body: Missouri's campus is 100 miles from the Enterprise Center in St. Louis. Miami traveled 1,200+ miles. The NCAA committee gave the 10-seed a de facto home game. This is one of the most lopsided geographic draws in the entire bracket.

history.callout: Missouri has been to 30+ NCAA Tournaments but has never reached the Final Four — the program's great unreached milestone. Miami reached the Final Four in 2023 under Jim Larrañaga, then cratered to 7-24 the next year. These teams have met once before: in 2002, when Missouri upset Miami in the first round and went on to the Elite Eight. Twenty-four years later, they meet again in St. Louis.

timeline.text: Norm Stewart's best team goes 14-0 in Big Eight play and reaches the Elite Eight — later vacated by NCAA.
As a 12-seed, Missouri upsets 5-seed Miami (FL) in the first round. Goes on to the Elite Eight before falling to Oklahoma.
Missouri goes 31-7, wins the Big 12 Tournament, and reaches the Elite Eight. Loses to UConn 82-75. The program's most recent deep run.
Under Dennis Gates, Missouri returns to the tournament for the first time since 2018. But they lose in the first round in both 2024 (to Drake) and 2025. Zero tournament wins in the Gates era.
Third tournament bid in four years under Gates. Playing 100 miles from campus in St. Louis. Mark Mitchell averaging 18.3 PPG. The pressure to finally break through is immense.

subsections.title: Miami's Rise, Fall, and Resurrection
subsections.content:
The Miami basketball story of the past three years is one of the wildest in college basketball. In April 2023, Jim Larrañaga led the Hurricanes to the Final Four as a 5-seed — beating top-seeded Houston and 2-seed Texas before losing to UConn. It was the program's greatest achievement and Larrañaga's second Final Four (after his legendary 2006 George Mason run).

Then everything collapsed. Eight players entered the transfer portal. The 2024-25 season was a disaster: 7-24, the worst in modern program history. Larrañaga, 75 years old and heartbroken by the portal, retired in December 2024 amid a 4-8 start. He said his heart was "simply no longer in the game."

Enter Jai Lucas, a 37-year-old first-time head coach with connections from Duke, Kentucky, and Texas. He rebuilt the entire roster in a single offseason, assembling a team of portal transfers and five-star freshmen around a "Monster" identity. The result: 25-8, ranked 25th in the AP Poll, and back in the NCAA Tournament. From ruins to resurrection in nine months.

subsections.title: The 7-vs-10 Matchup: The Coin Flip
subsections.content:
The 7-vs-10 game is one of the closest matchups in the NCAA Tournament. Historically, the 10-seed wins about 39% of the time — closer to a coin flip than any other major upset. The 10-seed has won in 6 of the last 8 tournaments (at least once). The reason: 7-seeds are often flawed teams with one clear weakness, while 10-seeds are talented teams with one clear reason the committee didn't rank them higher.

Missouri fits the 10-seed profile: an SEC team with NBA-level talent but defensive issues (237th in KenPom) and a maddening inability to string together wins. Miami fits the 7-seed profile: an impressive record and metrics (KenPom 31st) but questions about how a portal-assembled team handles tournament pressure. Add in Missouri's massive geographic advantage, and this game is genuinely unpredictable.
